title: Offices of Notaries

narrative.executive_view: 541120 is a narrow legal-document industry, not the ordinary mobile-notary or remote-online-notarization market. Census defines it as non-lawyer establishments empowered to draft, approve and execute legal documents, while assigning ordinary witnessing and signature certification to 541199 [S1]. Louisiana's civil-law notaries are the clearest domestic operating analogue: they may receive wills, conveyances and contracts, and their compliant acts are authentic acts [S2]. The corrected screen is still unattractive for a $1–10M EBITDA acquisition, but for a different reason: the addressable specialist-office population appears tiny and no code-specific evidence establishes a target-band company.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI can reduce document intake, first-draft, clause comparison, file organization, correspondence, scheduling and billing work. That maps to a meaningful share of the supplied 5411 legal-services proxy, especially paralegal and administrative roles, but it does not replace the state-authorized professional's approval, execution, recordkeeping or liability-bearing judgment. The ABA-hosted 2025 legal-industry survey reports uneven firm use of generative AI, including about 20% legal-specific AI implementation at firms with 50 or fewer lawyers, and use in drafting correspondence and operations [S4]. It is a legal-services proxy rather than a 541120 census.
narrative.value_capture: The corrected product is a regulated professional service, so an authorized civil-law notary can retain some savings from faster intake and drafting rather than being limited to a generic per-signature fee. Louisiana law grants authority over contracts, conveyances and wills [S2], and Florida requires a civil-law notary to be a Florida Bar member with five years' practice before appointment [S3]. That credential and legal accountability create some moat, but a small office faces templates, legal-document software, lawyers and referrals as alternatives; savings will be shared through lower fixed fees and faster turnaround. No ordinary-notary fee cap or RON-platform take rate is used here.
narrative.adoption_timing: The best available adoption evidence is a broad small-law-firm proxy, not a measure of 541120. The ABA-hosted survey found personal generative-AI use growing but only approximately 20% legal-specific AI implementation at firms with 50 or fewer lawyers, with ethics and workflow integration constraining deployment [S4]. I therefore estimate current effective adoption for the much smaller, credential-sensitive 541120 workflow below 50%, at 20%, and set a three-year path to 50%. This is deliberately slower than ordinary RON adoption because the relevant work includes legal-document approval and execution, not just identity verification.
narrative.consolidation_economics: There is no usable code-level establishment, revenue or transaction series in the supplied dataset, and its n_total is zero. The fallback is therefore a feasibility estimate, not a market statistic: use a deliberately generous 2,000 specialized U.S. offices, $0.25–1.0M annual revenue per office, and 20–30% EBITDA margins, yielding roughly $50k–$300k EBITDA for the modeled typical office. The $105-per-hour document-preparation price shown by one Louisiana civil-law-notary provider [S5] is only an illustrative service-price anchor, not a revenue benchmark. On that chain, estimated $1M-EBITDA firms are zero, with a 0–3 outlier range for unusual multi-office practices; lawyer offices and title/settlement businesses are excluded by classification rather than used to manufacture scale.
narrative.terminal_demand: Paid demand for authorized drafting, approval, execution and archival of legal documents is durable while state law reserves those acts to qualified people. Louisiana authorizes notaries to receive contracts, conveyances and wills [S2], and Florida ties civil-law-notary status to an experienced Bar member and grants authority to issue authentic acts [S3]. AI pressures labor time and routine-document pricing, but it does not itself hold the appointment, make an authentic act or bear the regulated professional role. The more credible terminal risk is margin compression and substitution toward lawyers, templates or software, not wholesale replacement by ordinary RON platforms.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: This code is unusually difficult to size. The supplied labor share is 5411-parent data and the supplied role mix contains lawyers even though Census excludes offices of lawyers and attorneys from 541120 [S1]. Louisiana and Florida statutes demonstrate relevant authority but do not measure a national commercial-office population; the Louisiana price guide is one provider rather than a representative survey [S5]. Accordingly, every market-size, ownership, multiple and target-band estimate is low confidence. The primary diligence question is whether a specific candidate is truly a 541120 specialist office rather than a lawyer practice, title/settlement operation or ordinary 541199 notary service.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau 2022 NAICS definition: 541120 Offices of Notaries
sources.what_it_supports: The assigned industry's legal-document drafting, approval and execution scope; the exclusion of ordinary witnessing-only notary-public services to 541199.

sources.title: Louisiana Revised Statutes 35:2, General powers of notaries public
sources.what_it_supports: A code-relevant civil-law-notary example: authority over wills, conveyances, contracts and authentic acts.

sources.title: Florida Statutes, Chapter 118 Civil-Law Notaries
sources.what_it_supports: Civil-law-notary qualifications, appointment, authentic-act authority and archival obligations; a second code-relevant state model.

sources.title: American Bar Association, The Legal Industry Report 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Broad legal-services proxy evidence on uneven generative-AI use, small-firm legal-specific implementation, drafting correspondence and operations.

sources.title: Louisiana Notary Service Price Guide
sources.what_it_supports: One provider's posted $105/hour document-preparation price, used only as an illustrative service-price anchor and not as industry-average revenue evidence.

n_band.rationale: Full feasibility chain, deliberately not a claimed census: estimate a generous 2,000 specialized 541120 offices nationally; use $0.25–1.0M revenue per office and a 20–30% EBITDA margin; modeled EBITDA is $50k–$300k per office, below $1M; therefore the selected estimated number of $1–10M EBITDA firms is 0. S1 confines the population to legal-document offices, while S2/S3 show the credentialed activity and S5 provides only an illustrative $105/hour document-preparation price. Lawyer offices, title/settlement operations and ordinary 541199 witnessing services are excluded rather than counted as 541120 scale.
caveats: No official national establishment, revenue, margin or transaction series was found for 541120; the 2,000-office population and revenue/margin assumptions are judgmental upper-bound inputs.
S5 is a single provider price list, not representative revenue, utilization or EBITDA evidence.
A rare multi-office specialist could exceed the modeled range; candidate-level diligence must verify NAICS scope before treating it as a target.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: The supplied 5411 role mix remains a low-quality parent proxy, but it is directionally closer after correcting the business to legal-document work. AI assists drafting and administration, while authorized approval, execution, archival and professional judgment remain human functions under the state models in S2 and S3.
caveats: Supplied roles and labor share are 5411-parent data, not a measured 541120 payroll mix; lawyers are explicitly excluded from 541120 by S1.
Fractions are task judgments, not observed headcount reductions or a claim that AI may execute regulated acts.

role_judgments.rationale: Lawyer-parent proxy only: drafting assistance can help, but legal judgment and responsibility remain.
Paralegal-style document assembly, retrieval and intake can be assisted with review.
Legal-secretary workflow, correspondence and routing are relatively automatable.
General clerical intake, indexing and status work are automation-friendly.
Routine questions, scheduling and confirmations can be automated, with exceptions retained.
Reconciliation, invoicing and bookkeeping can be automated with control review.
Supervision, exception handling and accountable control remain largely human.
General administrative correspondence and scheduling can be assisted substantially.
Search and record-preparation work can be assisted, but verification and legal effect remain human.
Management retains responsibility for quality, compliance and client relationships.
Residual parent-proxy work is assumed mixed administrative and professional support.

pi_dist.rationale: Credentialed document approval/execution permits a specialist office to retain part of workflow savings, but broad legal-service adoption and flat-fee pressure indicate that some savings will pass through to clients.
caveats: No 541120 pricing study exists; S4 is a small-law-firm proxy and S5 is one provider's price list.

pi_moat.rationale: The authority to draft, approve and execute documents, plus state-specific qualification and archival duties, is a real credential moat; limited geography, templates and substitute legal providers constrain it.
caveats: This estimates operator retention of savings, not survival of paid demand; legal-document software and lawyer competition are not quantified.

t50_years.rationale: The small-firm legal proxy reports approximately 20% legal-specific AI implementation and uneven uptake; regulated approval and execution work likely moves no faster, so a three-year estimate to 50% is used.
caveats: S4 does not measure 541120 and its survey population includes lawyers, who are excluded from this industry.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Use the approximately 20% legal-specific AI implementation reported for firms with 50 or fewer lawyers as a conservative proxy, not as an observed 541120 penetration rate.
caveats: The proxy may either overstate or understate specialist notary-office use; it establishes no code-level census.

historical_analogs.value: Small legal-service firms adopting document automation and AI-assisted correspondence while retaining regulated human review and execution.
historical_analogs.rationale: Legal-firm evidence supports an augmentation and workflow-compression analogue; the civil-law-notary authorization in S3 explains why it is not a full substitution analogue.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Adoption and price effects may be slower or faster than the legal-firm proxy.
caveats: No historical code-specific analogue was found; this is directional only.

selection.rationale: No source was found for 541120 owner age; retain a broad low-confidence placeholder rather than crediting a legal-services demographic as direct evidence.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No source documents a 541120 succession shortage, so none is credited.
caveats: Absence of located evidence is not evidence of no succession issue.

active_consolidators.rationale: No active acquirer of standalone 541120 legal-document offices was identified; ordinary RON platforms and title/settlement businesses are out of scope.
caveats: An undisclosed local roll-up could exist; no transaction database was available.

buy_mult.rationale: A scarce, key-person and unproven-target-market asset warrants a conservative placeholder purchase multiple pending actual transactions.
caveats: No reopened 541120 transaction comparable supports this value.

exit_mult.rationale: No demonstrated scaling or buyer universe supports exit expansion, so the terminal multiple equals the placeholder buy multiple.
caveats: No reopened 541120 transaction comparable supports this value.

pricing_structure.value: Negotiated professional-service fees for legal-document preparation, approval, execution and archival; no national per-act ordinary-notary fee cap assumed.
pricing_structure.rationale: The classification and state-law examples concern legal-document authority rather than a witnessing-only stamp; S5 illustrates time-based document-preparation pricing at one Louisiana provider.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Fees vary by jurisdiction, document complexity, professional qualification and client channel.
caveats: S5 is one provider and cannot establish national pricing or price elasticity.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Task assistance is distinct from adoption: the legal-services proxy supports administrative and drafting automation, while the 20% current-adoption estimate and three-year t50 reflect constrained deployment in credential-sensitive work.

terminal_value.justification: The paid service remains durable while authorized humans are needed to draft, approve, execute and archive legal documents; Louisiana and Florida law assign those acts and qualifications to notaries [S2][S3]. AI pressures time and price, which is captured in C rather than counted as terminal disappearance.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Ordinary notary-public witnessing, RON platforms, title/settlement offices and lawyer offices are excluded. Parent legal-services data are disclosed only as a labor/adoption proxy because the deterministic dataset provides no code-specific series.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Potential lower fixed fees and faster document turnaround reduce pi_dist; state-authorized legal-document execution preserves terminal demand and is not counted as task replaceability.

dataset_mismatch.description: Labor share and every supplied occupation are 5411-parent data; the role mix includes lawyers although Census excludes offices of lawyers and attorneys from 541120.
sensitivity: A true specialist-notary payroll could differ materially from the parent legal-services mix, changing V.
The n_band fallback is a low-confidence feasibility estimate because the dataset contains neither a usable count nor a size distribution.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Specialized non-lawyer offices empowered to draft, approve and execute legal documents
market_boundary.selection_basis: Census 541120 definition and its cross-references: ordinary witnessing-only notaries are 541199, and lawyer offices are 541110.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A is 5.0 at three years, ranges from 5.0 at two years due to the cap to about 1.67 at six years.
B is zero at the selected value; any nonzero outlier count would raise buyability modestly but would not establish an investable market.
The 5411-parent mismatch makes V directional only and could move it materially.

reviewer_note: Attempt 2 remediates the prior material scope failure. Every score-driving source was reopened on 2026-07-21. The packet analyzes only 541120 legal-document offices and treats ordinary notary/RON evidence as out of scope.
